single.brief_rationale: The single agent correctly implemented a train/test split and computed slice-level MAE, identifying the worst slices in the code output. However, the run failed catastrophically due to a Python syntax error during visualization generation, followed by a 500 Internal Server Error, preventing the delivery of final visualizations, the complete table, or any operational mitigations. The methodology was sound (avoiding leakage), but the execution was incomplete.

multi.brief_rationale: The multi-agent run successfully executed the audit pipeline, producing a ranked slice MAE table, validating statistical significance of sample sizes, and generating three out of four requested visualizations saved as artifacts. While the transcript ends before explicit text mitigations are output (flagged by the reviewer), the structured workflow (Planner, Researcher, Reviewer) ensured robust validation and artifact generation. The model was fit on full data (potential leakage for generalization error), but acceptable for a descriptive slice audit.

scores.comparison_notes: The multi-agent run is the clear winner because it successfully completed the core analysis and generated visual artifacts, whereas the single agent crashed before finishing. The multi-agent workflow demonstrated better resilience (recovering from encoding errors) and rigorous validation (sample size thresholds, reviewer checks). Although both transcripts lack the final text summary of operational mitigations, the multi-agent run produced the necessary analytical foundation (tables, charts, JSON) to support them, while the single agent produced nothing usable at the end due to the system error.
